topic: One Option (Abnormal / Developmental / Health / Human Relationships)
subject: psychology

items.q: What is the behaviourist view on the nature-nurture debate?
items.correct: Strongly nurture - we are shaped by environmental conditioning.
items.why: Behaviourists reject innate ideas (tabula rasa - blank slate). Students soften this to 'a mix'. Watson and Skinner explicitly attributed nearly all behaviour to environment.

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: The Robbers Cave study was conducted by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sherif. “Asch” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Buss (1989) surveyed how many cultures on mate preferences?
items.why: It's 37. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Hazan and Shaver publish adult attachment styles?
items.why: The real figure is 1987 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
